summary:
The Franco-Prussian War, fought from July 1870 to May 1871, was the decisive conflict that completed [[event:2534|German Unification]] under Prussian leadership and dealt a crushing blow to French imperial ambitions. The war originated in a dispute over the candidacy of a Hohenzollern prince — Prussia's royal family — for the vacant Spanish throne, which France viewed as an unacceptable encirclement. Bismarck exploited the crisis, editing the Ems Dispatch to make the Prussian king's response to French demands appear insulting, provoking France into declaring war first.

The Prussian and north and south German forces, now fighting together under unified command, demonstrated a decisive superiority in mobilization speed, artillery, and tactical coordination. French forces were defeated in rapid succession at the border battles of Worth and Gravelotte. The catastrophe at the [[event:3764|Battle of Sedan]] in September 1870 saw the capture of Emperor Napoleon III and an entire French army of over 100,000 men — an unprecedented humiliation that immediately brought down the Second Empire and led to the proclamation of the Third Republic in Paris.

However, the fall of the empire did not end the war. The new French government of National Defense organized continued resistance, and Paris withstood a German siege of over four months. Francs-tireurs (irregular fighters) harassed German supply lines, and provincial armies attempted to relieve the capital. The siege ended in January 1871 when Paris, wracked by famine, capitulated. The subsequent armistice was bitterly received by radical Parisians, whose resistance in the [[event:2560|Paris Commune]] of March–May 1871 resulted in the bloodiest episode of internal French violence in the nineteenth century.

The [[event:3765|Treaty of Frankfurt]] imposed harsh terms: France ceded Alsace and most of Lorraine — provinces with significant German-speaking populations but deep French cultural roots — and paid a massive indemnity of five billion francs. The loss of Alsace-Lorraine and the humiliation of the Versailles proclamation created a revanchist current in French politics that contributed to the dynamics leading to the First World War. The war also consolidated the south German states into the new German Empire and inaugurated a period of German continental dominance.
